result.markdown:
--- title: "Publication Bias Diagnostics" subtitle: "Small-Study Effects and Time-Lag Bias in Mate-Choice Copying (MCC) Meta-Analyses" author: "Eduardo S. A. Santos & Collaborators" date: today bibliography: ../../references.bib format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: cosmo self-contained: true --- ## Introduction & Methodology Publication bias is a critical threat to the validity of meta-analytic syntheses. If studies with statistically non-significant or small effects are systematically omitted from the literature, the pooled meta-analytic summary will be biased upward. To thoroughly evaluate the presence of publication bias and report-level selection in our Mate-Choice Copying (MCC) dataset, we employ three complementary diagnostic approaches, keeping the exact multilevel modeling logic (e.g., nested random intercepts and taxonomy control) used in our main overall effect calculations: 1. **Visual Inspection (Funnel Plots):** We plot effect size estimates against their precision (inverse of the standard error, $1/\text{SE}$) to check for visual asymmetry around the overall mean estimate. 2. **Egger's Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects):** We test whether study precision significantly moderates effect size. Following the recommendations of @nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022 and Stanley [-@stanleyMetaregressionAnalysisEconomics2012; -@stanleyMetaregressionApproximationsReduce2014], we implement a **two-step approach**: * We first fit the Egger regression model using the standard error ($\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}$) as the precision proxy. * If the intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant, it indicates that the estimate is downwardly biased. In this case, we re-fit the model using the sampling variance ($\text{V} = v_i$) as the moderator to obtain a less biased estimate of the adjusted overall meta-analytic mean. 3. **Decline Effect / Time-Lag Bias:** We test whether effect sizes shrink over time by adding publication year (mean-centered) to our models while simultaneously controlling for study precision. 4. **Bias-Corrected Meta-Analytic Means (Method Comparison):** Finally, we estimate the *adjusted* overall mean under two complementary correction frameworks and compare them directly. The **current** approach follows @nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022: the intercept of a multilevel meta-regression on the sampling variance ($v_i$) is interpreted as the mean when sampling error is zero (i.e., an infinitely precise study). The **new** approach follows @yangRobustPointVariance2024: a *bias-robust* weighting scheme (a fixed-/common-effect GLS estimator that is less susceptible to selective reporting) is combined with **cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation** to handle the non-independence of effect sizes within studies. We overlay both corrected means on an orchard plot using `orchaRd::pub_bias_plot()`. --- ## Data Loading and Alignment We read, clean, and align the new extractions and original datasets, extracting sample sizes and publication years to construct our precision and time-lag moderators. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Paths to datasets path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx") path_davies <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx") path_jones <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV") # A. Read raw spreadsheets db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction") db_davies_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data") db_jones_raw <- readr::read_csv(path_jones) # B. Clean new extractions db_new <- db_new_raw %>% filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>% mutate( effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance), logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance, identifierStudyYear = as.numeric(identifierStudyYear), controlN = as.numeric(controlN), treatmentN = as.numeric(treatmentN), effectSizesampleSize = as.numeric(effectSizesampleSize) ) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 1. Hedges' g Dataset Alignment (Continuous) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- hedges_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), !is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance)) %>% select( yi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD, vi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, Year = identifierStudyYear, controlN, treatmentN, totalN = effectSizesampleSize ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) hedges_davies <- db_davies_raw %>% mutate( Hedges_d_directional = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional), Variance = as.numeric(Variance), Year = as.numeric(Year), N_control = as.numeric(N_control), N_treatment = as.numeric(N_treatment), Total_n_analysis = as.numeric(Total_n_analysis) ) %>% filter(!is.na(Hedges_d_directional), !is.na(Variance), Variance > 0) %>% select( yi = Hedges_d_directional, vi = Variance, identifierStudyId = Author, identifierEffectSizeID = Effect_size_no, taxonomySpecies = Species_latin, Year, controlN = N_control, treatmentN = N_treatment, totalN = Total_n_analysis ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)" ) db_hedges <- bind_rows(hedges_new, hedges_davies) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # 2. Log Odds Ratio Dataset Alignment (Binary) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- or_new <- db_new %>% filter(!is.na(logOddsRatio), !is.na(logOddsRatioVariance)) %>% select( yi = logOddsRatio, vi = logOddsRatioVariance, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, Year = identifierStudyYear ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) or_jones <- db_jones_raw %>% filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>% select( yi = lnOR, vi = VlnOR, identifierStudyId = study, identifierEffectSizeID = study_Exp, taxonomySpecies = Species, Year = year ) %>% mutate( identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" ) db_or <- bind_rows(or_new, or_jones) %>% mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor)) ``` ::: --- ## Study Inclusion & Exclusion {#sec-inclusion-pb} The following tables document which effect sizes from the new extraction are included in the meta-analytic models and which have been removed, with the reason for each exclusion. ### Excluded Effect Sizes ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Included Effect Sizes ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## Diagnostics by Effect Size Class ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous outcomes) We fit multilevel models on the continuous combined dataset ($k = 227$) to run our diagnostics. #### 1. Baseline Model & Funnel Plot We run the intercept-only model to establish the global mean effect size and plot precision ($1/\text{SE}$) against the standardized residuals. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Intercept-only model VCV_hedges <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_hedges) ma_hedges <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_hedges, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_hedges ) summary(ma_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 227; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -259.1581 518.3162 526.3162 539.9983 526.4972 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0394 0.1985 80 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.3628 0.6023 227 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.0244 0.1562 31 no taxonomySpecies Test for Heterogeneity: Q(df = 226) = 1426.7546, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub 0.4566 0.0734 6.2204 226 <.0001 0.3120 0.6013 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Extract BLUPs for random effects blups <- ranef(ma_hedges) study_ef <- blups$identifierStudyId$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$identifierStudyId, row.names(blups$identifierStudyId))] obs_ef <- blups$identifierEffectSizeID$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$identifierEffectSizeID, row.names(blups$identifierEffectSizeID))] spec_ef <- blups$taxonomySpecies$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$taxonomySpecies, row.names(blups$taxonomySpecies))] # Handle any missing values by replacing with 0 study_ef[is.na(study_ef)] <- 0 obs_ef[is.na(obs_ef)] <- 0 spec_ef[is.na(spec_ef)] <- 0 # Marginal residuals: observed - fixed-effect prediction resid_marg <- db_hedges$yi - predict(ma_hedges)[[1]] # Conditional residuals: observed - fixed - random intercepts (study, obs, species) resid_cond <- db_hedges$yi - (predict(ma_hedges)[[1]] + study_ef + obs_ef + spec_ef) # Side-by-side funnel plots of marginal vs. conditional residuals, # with (a)/(b) panel tags. Drawn on-screen and saved to a manuscript PDF. draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_hedges$vi) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/hedges_baseline_and_funnel-1.png){width=1152} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} save_base_pdf( "funnel_hedges", function() draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_hedges$vi) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` quartz_off_screen 2 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note title="Reading the funnel plots"} Both panels plot each effect size against its precision ($1/\text{SE}$), but differ in what is removed before plotting: * **(a) Marginal residuals** are the deviations of each observed effect from the *fixed-effect (intercept) prediction* — the pooled mean — so they still carry all of the random-effects heterogeneity (between-study, between-effect-size, and between-species). Asymmetry here — a shortage of small, imprecise studies on one side of the funnel — is the classic visual signature of small-study and publication bias. Because genuine heterogeneity can mimic or exaggerate that asymmetry, apparent gaps in (a) should be read cautiously and confirmed against the formal Egger's regression below. * **(b) Conditional residuals** additionally subtract the estimated random intercepts (study, effect-size, and species BLUPs), so the heterogeneity captured by the multilevel structure is partialled out. A symmetric, evenly-filled funnel here means that once the nesting is accounted for there is little precision-related asymmetry left — i.e., the asymmetry seen in (a) is largely explained by the modelled random effects rather than by selective reporting. Any asymmetry that *persists* in (b) is the more credible visual indication of true small-study effects. ::: #### 2. Egger's Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects) To perform Egger's regression on Hedges' $g$, we follow the two-step framework recommended by @nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022 and Stanley [-@stanleyMetaregressionAnalysisEconomics2012; -@stanleyMetaregressionApproximationsReduce2014]. We use the sampling standard error ($\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}$) as our precision proxy. If this moderator is statistically significant ($p < 0.05$), it indicates a significant small-study effect (larger standard errors / smaller sample sizes associated with larger effect magnitudes). Because standard error model intercepts can be downwardly biased if they are significant, we apply a two-step correction: if the intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant, we re-fit the Egger's regression model using the sampling variance ($\text{V} = \text{vi}$) as the moderator to obtain a less biased estimate of the adjusted overall meta-analytic mean. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate standard error precision proxy db_hedges_egger <- db_hedges %>% mutate(se = sqrt(vi)) %>% filter(!is.na(se)) # Re-estimate VCV on this subset VCV_hedges_egger <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_hedges_egger) # Step 1: Egger regression model with standard error as moderator egger_hedges <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_hedges_egger, mods = ~ 1 + se, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_hedges_egger ) summary(egger_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 227; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -231.6564 463.3128 473.3128 490.3933 473.5868 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0604 0.2459 80 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.2763 0.5256 227 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.0282 0.1680 31 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 225) = 1302.8458, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficient 2): F(df1 = 1, df2 = 225) = 57.1174, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt -0.9327 0.1978 -4.7151 225 <.0001 -1.3224 -0.5429 *** se 4.3580 0.5766 7.5576 225 <.0001 3.2217 5.4942 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Step 2: Since the intercept is significant, re-fit using variance (vi) egger_hedges_v <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_hedges_egger, mods = ~ 1 + vi, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_hedges_egger ) summary(egger_hedges_v) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 227; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -223.7310 447.4620 457.4620 474.5425 457.7360 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0448 0.2117 80 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.2643 0.5141 227 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.0167 0.1294 31 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 225) = 1249.0779, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficient 2): F(df1 = 1, df2 = 225) = 74.8364, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt -0.2130 0.1012 -2.1054 225 0.0364 -0.4124 -0.0136 * vi 6.0481 0.6991 8.6508 225 <.0001 4.6704 7.4258 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ::: We visualize the Egger regression using a bubble plot. Effect sizes are plotted as circles scaled by precision, with the predicted regression line showing the relationship between sample size and effect size. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_egger_hedges <- orchaRd::bubble_plot( egger_hedges, mod = "se", group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Standard Error of Hedges' g (se)", ylab = "Effect size (Hedges' g)" ) # Color points by real source p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$data$real_source <- db_hedges_egger$Dataset_Source p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill p_egger_hedges <- p_egger_hedges + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Egger's Meta-Regression (Hedges' g)") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_egger_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/hedges_egger_bubble_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The slope for standard error (`se`) is **positive** ($\beta = 4.36$, $SE = 0.58$, $t = 7.56$, $p < 0.0001$), indicating clear evidence of small-study effects. > > **Two-Step Correction:** The intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant ($p < 0.0001$). Following the two-step recommendation of @nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022 and Stanley [-@stanleyMetaregressionAnalysisEconomics2012; -@stanleyMetaregressionApproximationsReduce2014], we re-fit Egger's regression using the sampling variance (`vi`) as the moderator. In this corrected model, the adjusted overall meta-analytic mean is estimated at $\beta_0 = -0.21$ ($SE = 0.10$, $t = -2.11$, $p = 0.036$). #### 3. Decline Effect (Time-Lag Bias) We analyze the decline effect by evaluating whether the publication year significantly predicts the effect size, while simultaneously controlling for the study sample size precision proxy. We mean-center the year so the intercept represents the effect in an average publication year. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Mean-center publication year db_hedges_decline <- db_hedges_egger %>% mutate(Year_c = Year - mean(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) # VCV for decline subset VCV_hedges_decline <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_hedges_decline) # Time-lag decline model controlling for sampling variance (vi) decline_hedges <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_hedges_decline, mods = ~ 1 + Year_c + vi, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_hedges_decline ) summary(decline_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 227; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -222.9296 445.8592 457.8592 478.3291 458.2463 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0461 0.2147 80 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.2647 0.5145 227 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.0190 0.1380 31 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 224) = 1239.3706, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficients 2:3): F(df1 = 2, df2 = 224) = 37.3650, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt -0.2146 0.1041 -2.0612 224 0.0404 -0.4198 -0.0094 * Year_c -0.0019 0.0063 -0.3043 224 0.7612 -0.0144 0.0106 vi 6.0341 0.7187 8.3964 224 <.0001 4.6179 7.4503 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ::: We construct the bubble plot showing the decline effect over years: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_decline_hedges <- orchaRd::bubble_plot( decline_hedges, mod = "Year_c", group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Publication Year (centered)", ylab = "Effect size (Hedges' g)" ) # Color points by real source p_decline_hedges$layers[[1]]$data$real_source <- db_hedges_decline$Dataset_Source p_decline_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_decline_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill p_decline_hedges <- p_decline_hedges + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") # Relabel centered x-axis to actual calendar years mean_year_hedges <- mean(db_hedges_egger$Year, na.rm = TRUE) target_years_hedges <- seq(2000, 2026, by = 5) p_decline_hedges_relabeled <- p_decline_hedges + ggplot2::scale_x_continuous( name = "Publication Year", breaks = target_years_hedges - mean_year_hedges, labels = target_years_hedges ) + ggplot2::ggtitle("Decline Effect / Time-Lag Bias (Hedges' g)") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_decline_hedges_relabeled) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/hedges_decline_bubble_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The publication year slope is **non-significant** ($\beta = 0.00$, $SE = 0.01$, $t = -0.30$, $p = 0.761$), indicating little evidence of a decline effect over time in continuous outcomes. #### 4. Unified Diagnostic Dashboard (Hedges' $g$) We combine the Egger regression and publication year time-lag bubble plots into a single visual panel using `patchwork`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dashboard_hedges <- (p_egger_hedges / p_decline_hedges_relabeled) + patchwork::plot_annotation( title = "Publication Bias Diagnostic Dashboard — Hedges' g", tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")" ) & ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.tag = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12, face = "bold") ) print(dashboard_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/hedges_unified_dashboard-1.png){width=960} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} save_gg_pdf(dashboard_hedges, "dashboard_eggers_timelag_hedges", width = 10, height = 12) ``` ::: --- ### Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes) We fit multilevel models on the binary combined dataset ($k = 172$) to evaluate bias in Log Odds Ratio ($\ln\text{OR}$) studies. #### 1. Baseline Model & Funnel Plot We run the intercept-only model for Odds Ratio and inspect the funnel plot representing log Odds Ratio values plotted against their precision. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Intercept-only model VCV_or <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_or) ma_or <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_or, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_or ) summary(ma_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 172; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -229.2388 458.4776 466.4776 479.0443 466.7186 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0339 0.1840 69 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.3542 0.5951 172 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.1920 0.4382 29 no taxonomySpecies Test for Heterogeneity: Q(df = 171) = 611.9869, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub 0.5999 0.1377 4.3553 171 <.0001 0.3280 0.8718 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Extract BLUPs for random effects blups <- ranef(ma_or) study_ef <- blups$identifierStudyId$intrcpt[match(db_or$identifierStudyId, row.names(blups$identifierStudyId))] obs_ef <- blups$identifierEffectSizeID$intrcpt[match(db_or$identifierEffectSizeID, row.names(blups$identifierEffectSizeID))] spec_ef <- blups$taxonomySpecies$intrcpt[match(db_or$taxonomySpecies, row.names(blups$taxonomySpecies))] # Handle any missing values by replacing with 0 study_ef[is.na(study_ef)] <- 0 obs_ef[is.na(obs_ef)] <- 0 spec_ef[is.na(spec_ef)] <- 0 # Marginal residuals: observed - fixed-effect prediction resid_marg <- db_or$yi - predict(ma_or)[[1]] # Conditional residuals: observed - fixed - random intercepts (study, obs, species) resid_cond <- db_or$yi - (predict(ma_or)[[1]] + study_ef + obs_ef + spec_ef) # Side-by-side funnel plots of marginal vs. conditional residuals, # with (a)/(b) panel tags. Drawn on-screen and saved to a manuscript PDF. draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_or$vi) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/or_baseline_and_funnel-1.png){width=1152} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} save_base_pdf( "funnel_or", function() draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_or$vi) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` quartz_off_screen 2 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-note title="Reading the funnel plots"} Both panels plot each $\ln\text{OR}$ effect size against its precision ($1/\text{SE}$), but differ in what is removed before plotting: * **(a) Marginal residuals** are the deviations of each observed effect from the *fixed-effect (intercept) prediction* — the pooled mean — so they still carry all of the random-effects heterogeneity (between-study, between-effect-size, and between-species). Asymmetry here — a shortage of small, imprecise studies on one side of the funnel — is the classic visual signature of small-study and publication bias. Because genuine heterogeneity can mimic or exaggerate that asymmetry, apparent gaps in (a) should be read cautiously and confirmed against the formal Egger's regression below. * **(b) Conditional residuals** additionally subtract the estimated random intercepts (study, effect-size, and species BLUPs), so the heterogeneity captured by the multilevel structure is partialled out. A symmetric, evenly-filled funnel here means that once the nesting is accounted for there is little precision-related asymmetry left — i.e., the asymmetry seen in (a) is largely explained by the modelled random effects rather than by selective reporting. Any asymmetry that *persists* in (b) is the more credible visual indication of true small-study effects. ::: #### 2. Egger's Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects) For Odds Ratio studies, we use the standard error ($\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}$) as our precision proxy in the two-step framework. If the standard error moderator slope is statistically significant ($p < 0.05$), it represents a significant small-study effect. As with the continuous outcomes, we apply the two-step correction: if the intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant, we re-fit the Egger's regression model using the sampling variance ($\text{V} = \text{vi}$) as the moderator to obtain a less biased estimate of the adjusted overall meta-analytic mean. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate standard error proxy db_or_egger <- db_or %>% mutate(se = sqrt(vi)) %>% filter(!is.na(se)) # Re-estimate VCV on this subset VCV_or_egger <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_or_egger) # Step 1: Egger regression model with standard error as moderator egger_or <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_or_egger, mods = ~ 1 + se, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_or_egger ) summary(egger_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 172; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -216.8556 433.7111 443.7111 459.3901 444.0770 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0084 0.0918 69 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.3207 0.5663 172 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.2114 0.4598 29 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 170) = 597.1982, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficient 2): F(df1 = 1, df2 = 170) = 22.5256, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt -0.5716 0.2806 -2.0369 170 0.0432 -1.1256 -0.0176 * se 2.1147 0.4456 4.7461 170 <.0001 1.2352 2.9943 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Step-2: Since the intercept is significant, re-fit using variance (vi) egger_or_v <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_or_egger, mods = ~ 1 + vi, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_or_egger ) summary(egger_or_v) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 172; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -216.4096 432.8192 442.8192 458.4982 443.1850 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0000 0.0001 69 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.3252 0.5703 172 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.1502 0.3876 29 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 170) = 586.7741, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficient 2): F(df1 = 1, df2 = 170) = 23.5751, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt 0.1001 0.1617 0.6192 170 0.5366 -0.2190 0.4193 vi 1.4198 0.2924 4.8554 170 <.0001 0.8425 1.9970 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ::: We visualize the Egger regression for Odds Ratio using a bubble plot. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_egger_or <- orchaRd::bubble_plot( egger_or, mod = "se", group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Standard Error of Log Odds Ratio (se)", ylab = "Effect size (lnOR)" ) # Color points by real source p_egger_or$layers[[1]]$data$real_source <- db_or_egger$Dataset_Source p_egger_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_egger_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill p_egger_or <- p_egger_or + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::ggtitle("Egger's Meta-Regression (Log Odds Ratio)") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_egger_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/or_egger_bubble_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The slope for the standard error is **positive** ($\beta = 2.11$, $SE = 0.45$, $t = 4.75$, $p < 0.0001$), indicating pronounced small-study effects in binary mate-choice copying outcomes. > > **Two-Step Correction:** The intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant ($p = 0.043$). Following the two-step framework, we re-fit the model using the sampling variance (`vi`) as the moderator to obtain a less biased estimate of the adjusted mean. In this corrected model, the adjusted overall log odds ratio is non-significant at $\beta_0 = 0.10$ ($SE = 0.16$, $t = 0.62$, $p = 0.537$). #### 3. Decline Effect (Time-Lag Bias) We test for a decline effect in the binary outcomes by regressing $\ln\text{OR}$ on centered publication year while simultaneously controlling for standard errors. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Mean-center publication year db_or_decline <- db_or_egger %>% mutate(Year_c = Year - mean(Year, na.rm = TRUE)) # VCV for decline subset VCV_or_decline <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_or_decline) # Time-lag decline model controlling for sampling variance (vi) decline_or <- metafor::rma.mv( yi = yi, V = VCV_or_decline, mods = ~ 1 + Year_c + vi, random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = db_or_decline ) summary(decline_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Multivariate Meta-Analysis Model (k = 172; method: REML) logLik Deviance AIC BIC AICc -212.0418 424.0836 436.0836 454.8630 436.6022 Variance Components: estim sqrt nlvls fixed factor sigma^2.1 0.0000 0.0000 69 no identifierStudyId sigma^2.2 0.3154 0.5616 172 no identifierEffectSizeID sigma^2.3 0.2490 0.4990 29 no taxonomySpecies Test for Residual Heterogeneity: QE(df = 169) = 586.6984, p-val < .0001 Test of Moderators (coefficients 2:3): F(df1 = 2, df2 = 169) = 15.0175, p-val < .0001 Model Results: estimate se tval df pval ci.lb ci.ub intrcpt 0.0726 0.1772 0.4097 169 0.6825 -0.2772 0.4224 Year_c -0.0279 0.0105 -2.6661 169 0.0084 -0.0485 -0.0072 ** vi 1.3189 0.3011 4.3800 169 <.0001 0.7245 1.9134 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 ``` ::: ::: We construct the bubble plot showing the decline effect for Odds Ratios: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_decline_or <- orchaRd::bubble_plot( decline_or, mod = "Year_c", group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Publication Year (centered)", ylab = "Effect size (lnOR)" ) # Color points by real source p_decline_or$layers[[1]]$data$real_source <- db_or_decline$Dataset_Source p_decline_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour p_decline_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill p_decline_or <- p_decline_or + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") + ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") # Relabel centered x-axis to actual calendar years mean_year_or <- mean(db_or_egger$Year, na.rm = TRUE) target_years_or <- seq(2000, 2026, by = 5) p_decline_or_relabeled <- p_decline_or + ggplot2::scale_x_continuous( name = "Publication Year", breaks = target_years_or - mean_year_or, labels = target_years_or ) + ggplot2::ggtitle("Decline Effect / Time-Lag Bias (Log Odds Ratio)") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_decline_or_relabeled) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/or_decline_bubble_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The publication year slope is **statistically significant** ($\beta = -0.03$, $SE = 0.01$, $t = -2.67$, $p = 0.008$). This provides evidence of a **decline effect (Time-Lag Bias)** in binary outcomes—the reported strength of mate-choice copying has decreased over the course of academic publication history. #### 4. Unified Diagnostic Dashboard (Log Odds Ratio) We combine the Egger regression and publication year time-lag bubble plots into a single visual panel using `patchwork`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dashboard_or <- (p_egger_or / p_decline_or_relabeled) + patchwork::plot_annotation( title = "Publication Bias Diagnostic Dashboard — Log Odds Ratio", tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")" ) & ggplot2::theme( plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), plot.tag = ggplot2::element_text(size = 12, face = "bold") ) print(dashboard_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/or_unified_dashboard-1.png){width=960} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} save_gg_pdf(dashboard_or, "dashboard_eggers_timelag_or", width = 10, height = 12) ``` ::: ::: --- ## Bias-Corrected Meta-Analytic Means: Current vs. New Method The diagnostics above *detect* small-study and time-lag bias. Here we turn to *correcting* the pooled mean, and we directly compare two correction philosophies on the same data. * **Current method — Nakagawa et al. [-@nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022] regression correction.** We re-use the multilevel meta-regression that includes the sampling variance ($v_i$) as a moderator (the Step-2 models `egger_hedges_v` / `egger_or_v` fitted above). Its **intercept** estimates the meta-analytic mean extrapolated to $v_i = 0$ — i.e., the effect a hypothetical infinitely-precise (infinite-$n$) study would report, with the small-study component removed. This is the *"bias-corrected estimate"* in @yangRobustPointVariance2024's terminology and in `orchaRd`. * **New method — Yang et al. [-@yangRobustPointVariance2024] bias-robust estimation.** A two-step procedure: (1) fit a **fixed-/common-effect** model, whose precision-based GLS weighting is far less inflated by selectively-reported small studies than the random-effects mean; then (2) apply **cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation** (`clubSandwich`), clustering by study, so that the standard errors and confidence intervals remain valid under the non-independence of multiple effect sizes per study. This is the *"bias-robust estimate"*. We compute both, overlay them on an orchard plot of the uncorrected multilevel mean with `orchaRd::pub_bias_plot()`, and tabulate the three estimates (uncorrected vs. the two corrections) side by side. ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Hedges' $g$ (Continuous outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- New method: Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate --------------------- # Step 1: common-/fixed-effect model (bias-robust GLS weighting). fe_hedges <- metafor::rma( yi = yi, vi = vi, data = db_hedges_egger, method = "FE", test = "t" ) # Step 2: cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation, clustering by study, # to account for non-independence of multiple effect sizes per study. fe_hedges_robust <- metafor::robust( fe_hedges, cluster = db_hedges_egger$identifierStudyId, clubSandwich = TRUE ) summary(fe_hedges_robust) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Fixed-Effects Model (k = 227) logLik deviance AIC BIC AICc -499.4348 1119.8276 1000.8695 1004.2945 1000.8873 I^2 (total heterogeneity / total variability): 79.82% H^2 (total variability / sampling variability): 4.95 Test for Heterogeneity: Q(df = 226) = 1119.8276, p-val < .0001 Number of estimates: 227 Number of clusters: 80 Estimates per cluster: 1-12 (mean: 2.84, median: 2) Model Results: estimate se¹ tval¹ df¹ pval¹ ci.lb¹ ci.ub¹ 0.3445 0.0490 7.0335 32.83 <.0001 0.2449 0.4442 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 1) results based on cluster-robust inference (var-cov estimator: CR2, approx t-test and confidence interval, df: Satterthwaite approx) ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # `egger_hedges_v` (fitted above) is the current-method Nakagawa et al. (2022) # vi-moderator model; its intercept is the bias-corrected mean at vi = 0. ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Orchard plot of the uncorrected multilevel meta-analytic mean ... oc_hedges <- orchaRd::orchard_plot( ma_hedges, group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Hedges' g", angle = 0 ) # ... with both corrected means overlaid: # red = Yang et al. 2024 "Bias Robust" (fixed-effect + CR2 robust) # blue = Nakagawa et al. 2022 "Bias Corrected" (vi-moderator intercept) p_pubbias_hedges <- orchaRd::pub_bias_plot( oc_hedges, fe_model = fe_hedges_robust, v_model = egger_hedges_v ) + ggplot2::ggtitle("Bias-Corrected Means — Hedges' g") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_pubbias_hedges) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/hedges_pub_bias_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The uncorrected multilevel mean is $\hat\mu = 0.46$ (95% CI [0.31, 0.60]). The **current** Nakagawa correction gives a bias-corrected mean of $-0.21$ (95% CI [-0.41, -0.01]), and the **new** Yang bias-robust estimate is $0.34$ (95% CI [0.24, 0.44]). ### Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # --- New method: Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate --------------------- fe_or <- metafor::rma( yi = yi, vi = vi, data = db_or_egger, method = "FE", test = "t" ) fe_or_robust <- metafor::robust( fe_or, cluster = db_or_egger$identifierStudyId, clubSandwich = TRUE ) summary(fe_or_robust) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Fixed-Effects Model (k = 172) logLik deviance AIC BIC AICc -313.6816 536.9996 629.3633 632.5108 629.3868 I^2 (total heterogeneity / total variability): 68.16% H^2 (total variability / sampling variability): 3.14 Test for Heterogeneity: Q(df = 171) = 536.9996, p-val < .0001 Number of estimates: 172 Number of clusters: 69 Estimates per cluster: 1-17 (mean: 2.49, median: 2) Model Results: estimate se¹ tval¹ df¹ pval¹ ci.lb¹ ci.ub¹ 0.5744 0.0726 7.9112 20.24 <.0001 0.4230 0.7257 *** --- Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 1) results based on cluster-robust inference (var-cov estimator: CR2, approx t-test and confidence interval, df: Satterthwaite approx) ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # `egger_or_v` (fitted above) is the current-method Nakagawa et al. (2022) # vi-moderator model; its intercept is the bias-corrected mean at vi = 0. ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} oc_or <- orchaRd::orchard_plot( ma_or, group = "identifierStudyId", xlab = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", angle = 0 ) p_pubbias_or <- orchaRd::pub_bias_plot( oc_or, fe_model = fe_or_robust, v_model = egger_or_v ) + ggplot2::ggtitle("Bias-Corrected Means — Log Odds Ratio") + ggplot2::theme_minimal() print(p_pubbias_or) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](publication_bias_files/figure-html/or_pub_bias_plot-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: > [!NOTE] > The uncorrected multilevel mean is $\hat\mu = 0.60$ (95% CI [0.33, 0.87]) on the $\ln\text{OR}$ scale. The **current** Nakagawa correction gives $0.10$ (95% CI [-0.22, 0.42]), and the **new** Yang bias-robust estimate is $0.57$ (95% CI [0.42, 0.73]). ::: ### Side-by-Side Comparison Table ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to read the comparison.** Both corrections should pull the pooled estimate *toward zero* relative to the uncorrected multilevel mean if small-study/selective-reporting bias is present; the `Pct_Change_vs_Uncorrected` column quantifies that shrinkage. The two methods answer slightly different questions. The **Nakagawa** intercept is a *model-based extrapolation* to a perfectly precise study and tends to give wide confidence intervals (it is an out-of-range prediction). The **Yang** bias-robust estimate stays within the observed data, down-weights heterogeneity-driven inflation through fixed-effect weighting, and uses cluster-robust CR2 standard errors that explicitly respect the within-study dependence — making it a more conservative, inference-valid sensitivity analysis. Agreement between the two corrections strengthens confidence in the adjusted conclusion; divergence flags that the corrected mean is sensitive to modelling assumptions. --- ## Methodological Synthesis & Discussion We compile the results of the small-study effect regressions and time-lag meta-regressions for both effect size classes below: ### Publication Bias Comparison Table ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Main Takeaways 1. **Small-Study Effects:** The Egger's regressions indicate statistically significant small-study effects for Hedges' $g$ ($p < 0.0001$) and statistically significant small-study effects for Log Odds Ratio ($p < 0.0001$). Where present, smaller and less precise trials systematically report larger mate-choice copying effect sizes—a classic signature of publication selection, in which small-sample studies are more likely to be published or reported if they find large, hypothesis-supportive effects. 2. **Decline Effect (Time-Lag Bias) in Binary Outcomes:** The publication-year trend for binary outcomes is statistically significant ($\beta = -0.03$, $p = 0.008$). Over the last two decades, the reported strength of mate-choice copying (measured by Log Odds Ratios) has decreased, a pattern typical of fields experiencing a novelty effect in which early breakthrough publications report large effect sizes and subsequent rigorous replications reveal more moderate, realistic estimates. 3. **Time Trend in Continuous Outcomes:** For Hedges' $g$ (continuous), the publication-year trend is non-significant ($\beta = 0.00$, $p = 0.761$). This suggests the continuous reporting scales have been relatively stable in magnitude over the history of the literature. 4. **Bias-Corrected Means — Current vs. New Method:** We compared two correction frameworks on the adjusted pooled mean. For Hedges' $g$, the **current** Nakagawa [-@nakagawaMethodsTestingPublication2022] $v_i$-intercept correction yields $-0.21$ while the **new** Yang [-@yangRobustPointVariance2024] bias-robust (fixed-effect + CR2) estimate yields $0.34$ (uncorrected multilevel mean: $0.46$). For Log Odds Ratio, the corresponding values are $0.10$ (Nakagawa) and $0.57$ (Yang), against an uncorrected mean of $0.60$. The Yang bias-robust estimate is generally the more conservative, inference-valid option because it respects within-study dependence through cluster-robust variance estimation and avoids the wide-interval extrapolation inherent in the regression-intercept approach; where the two corrections agree, the adjusted conclusion is robust to modelling choice. These diagnostic results suggest that while mate-choice copying is a genuine ecological phenomenon, the overall raw pooled estimates from both continuous and binary literatures may be moderately inflated due to selective reporting and publication biases. Future research should prioritize large-sample pre-registered replication studies to provide unbiased baselines. --- ## Session Info ::: callout-note ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sessionInfo() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24) Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin23 Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 Matrix products: default BLAS: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;
